title: Understand: nuclear fusion
description: Check your understanding of nuclear fusion in this set of free practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
In 1958, scientists working at Los Alamos National Laboratory created the first controlled fusion reaction, reaching temperatures around 15 million degrees Celsius! 

**What happens during nuclear fusion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Smaller nuclei are combined to make larger nuclei.
An unstable nucleus is bombarded by neutrons until it splits into two daughter nuclei.
A proton turns into a neutron and emits a positron.
Two hydrogen atoms are combined to form an $\ce{H_2}$ molecule.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the statement.**

In the process of nuclear fusion, [[☃ dropdown 1]] nuclei are [[☃ dropdown 2]] to make [[☃ dropdown 3]] nuclei.
hints: The process of nuclear fusion begins with smaller nuclei.
These smaller nuclei are combined together.
So in the process of nuclear fusion, smaller nuclei are combined to make larger nuclei.

choices.content: smaller
larger
combined
divided
smaller
larger

assessment_items.question_content:
Scientists hope that one day humans will use nuclear fusion as a source of safe and inexpensive power on Earth. 

**What happens during nuclear fusion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A larger nucleus is made from smaller nuclei.
The heat produced by the decay of radioisotopes is converted to electricity.
Matter and antimatter are brought together and converted to energy.
Larger nuclei are split into smaller nuclei.

assessment_items.question_content:
Stars, such as the sun, are primarily made from hydrogen. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the primary source of the sun’s energy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: “[Sun Emits a Solstice CME](https://images.nasa.gov/details/GSFC_20171208_Archive_e001434)” by NASA Goddard, [NASA Public Domain](https://www.nasa.gov/multimedia/guidelines/index.html)

options.alt: A NASA photograph of the sun showing the violence of its surface as it emits a solar flare.
options.caption: *The sun emitting a solar flare*

choices.content: Hydrogen nuclei fusing to form helium nuclei.
Hydrogen atoms breaking down into protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Hydrogen atoms combining to form $\ce{H_2}$ molecules.

assessment_items.question_content:
Stars produce elements through nuclear fusion. This process releases a tremendous amount of energy.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following best explains how the process of fusion works?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A photograph of the Pleiades star cluster in the constellation of Taurus.
options.caption: *The Pleiades cluster of stars*

choices.content: Smaller nuclei such as hydrogen are combined into larger nuclei such as helium.
Larger nuclei such as carbon are split into smaller nuclei such as helium.
A proton captures a free electron and forms an atom of hydrogen.

assessment_items.question_content:
Our sun is about 4.6 billion years old, and roughly halfway through its life. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the primary source of the sun’s energy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]


[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: An ultraviolet image of the sun taken by NASA's Solar Dynamics Observatory. This images reveals the violent surface of the sun.
options.caption: *A UV image of the sun*

choices.content: Hydrogen nuclei fusing into helium nuclei.
Hydrogen gas combusting with oxygen gas.
Hydrogen nuclei being annihilated by their anti-matter counterparts.

assessment_items.question_content:
The sun is powered by nuclear fusion. This process takes place over three main reactions. The equations are shown out of order below.

**Drag the equations to place them in the correct order from top to bottom.**

[[☃ sorter 2]]
hints: The sun is powered by fusing hydrogen nuclei into helium nuclei.

There are three steps to the reaction, so each equation should bring us closer to the final product. And because this is a *sequence* of reactions, the products of one reaction will be the reactants of the next.

Let’s determine the first reaction. Fusion starts with hydrogen, so our reaction can only have hydrogen as the reactant. Two reactions meet this requirement:

$\ce{^{1}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}  \rightarrow \ce{^{2}_{1}H} + \ce{^{0}_{+1}e} + \nu_e$

and 

$\ce{^{2}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}  \rightarrow \ce{^{3}_{2}He} +  \gamma$

Let’s see if we can spot any differences. Notice that the first reaction produces $\ce{H-2}$ as a *product*, and the second reaction uses that same isotope as a *reactant*. So the correct order of these two reactions must be:

$\ce{^{1}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}  \rightarrow \ce{^{2}_{1}H} + \ce{^{0}_{+1}e} + \nu_e$

followed by

$\ce{^{2}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}  \rightarrow \ce{^{3}_{2}He} +  \gamma$

There’s only one more reaction remaining:

 $\ce{^{3}_{2}He} + \ce{^{3}_{2}He}  \rightarrow \ce{^{4}_{2}He} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}$

See how the reactants in this equation include two $\ce{He-3}$  isotopes? These isotopes are produced by the second reaction (shown in the previous hint), which allows them to be used as reactants in the equation above.

assessment_items.question_content:
Our sun is an average-sized star, and is powered by nuclear fusion. This process takes place over three main reactions. The equations are shown out of order below.

**Drag the equations to place them in the correct order from top to bottom.**

[[☃ sorter 2]]
hints: The sun is powered by fusing hydrogen nuclei into helium nuclei.

There are three steps to the reaction, so each equation should bring us closer to the final product. And because this is a *sequence* of reactions, the products of one reaction will be the reactants of the next.

Let’s determine the first reaction. Fusion starts with hydrogen, so our reaction can only have hydrogen as the reactant. Two reactions meet this requirement:

$\ce{^{1}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}  \rightarrow \ce{^{2}_{1}H} + \ce{^{0}_{+1}e} + \nu_e$

and 

$\ce{^{2}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}  \rightarrow \ce{^{3}_{2}He} +  \gamma$

Let’s see if we can spot any differences. Notice that the first reaction produces $\ce{H-2}$ as a *product*, and the second reaction uses that same isotope as a *reactant*. So the correct order of these two reactions must be:

$\ce{^{1}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}  \rightarrow \ce{^{2}_{1}H} + \ce{^{0}_{+1}e} + \nu_e$

followed by

$\ce{^{2}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}  \rightarrow \ce{^{3}_{2}He} +  \gamma$

There’s only one more reaction remaining:

 $\ce{^{3}_{2}He} + \ce{^{3}_{2}He}  \rightarrow \ce{^{4}_{2}He} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}$

See how the reactants in this equation include two $\ce{He-3}$  isotopes? These isotopes are produced by the second reaction (shown in the previous hint), which allows them to be used as reactants in the equation above.

assessment_items.question_content:
All the light and heat we receive from the sun is the result of nuclear fusion. This process takes place over three main reactions. The equations are shown out of order below.

**Drag the equations to place them in the correct order from top to bottom.**

[[☃ sorter 2]]
hints: The sun is powered by fusing hydrogen nuclei into helium nuclei.

There are three steps to the reaction, so each equation should bring us closer to the final product. And because this is a *sequence* of reactions, the products of one reaction will be the reactants of the next.

Let’s determine the first reaction. Fusion starts with hydrogen, so our reaction can only have hydrogen as the reactant. Two reactions meet this requirement:

$\ce{^{1}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}  \rightarrow \ce{^{2}_{1}H} + \ce{^{0}_{+1}e} + \nu_e$

and 

$\ce{^{2}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}  \rightarrow \ce{^{3}_{2}He} +  \gamma$

Let’s see if we can spot any differences. Notice that the first reaction produces $\ce{H-2}$ as a *product*, and the second reaction uses that same isotope as a *reactant*. So the correct order of these two reactions must be:

$\ce{^{1}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}  \rightarrow \ce{^{2}_{1}H} + \ce{^{0}_{+1}e} + \nu_e$

followed by

$\ce{^{2}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}  \rightarrow \ce{^{3}_{2}He} +  \gamma$

There’s only one more reaction remaining:

 $\ce{^{3}_{2}He} + \ce{^{3}_{2}He}  \rightarrow \ce{^{4}_{2}He} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H} + \ce{^{1}_{1}H}$

See how the reactants in this equation include two $\ce{He-3}$  isotopes? These isotopes are produced by the second reaction (shown in the previous hint), which allows them to be used as reactants in the equation above.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two students are asked to explain how the Big Bang and stars produced the elements, a process called nucleosynthesis. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which one of these explanations is correct?**

[[☃ radio 1]]


[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: “[Hubble Spies Big Bang Frontiers](https://images.nasa.gov/details/GSFC_20171208_Archive_e000528)” by NASA Goddard, [NASA Public Domain](https://www.nasa.gov/multimedia/guidelines/index.html)

options.alt: A composite image of early galaxies photographed by the Hubble Telescope.
options.caption: *Early galaxies as seen by Hubble Telescope*

choices.content: **Student A:** The Big Bang created hydrogen, which formed stars. Stars fuse hydrogen together to make larger elements.
**Student B:** The Big Bang created all the elements. Stars break larger elements down into smaller elements until only hydrogen remains.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two students are asked to explain how the Big Bang and stars are related to nucleosynthesis, the formation of elements. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which one of these explanations is correct?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: “[Space Science](https://images.nasa.gov/details/0203045)” by Marshall Space Flight Center, [NASA Public Domain](https://www.nasa.gov/multimedia/guidelines/index.html)

options.alt: An image with a black background and a fluffy, bubble like structure with separate smaller structures surrounding it.
options.caption: *NASA artist’s conception of the early universe*

choices.content: **Student A:** Hydrogen was first created by the Big Bang. This hydrogen then formed stars, which fuse hydrogen into larger elements.
**Student B:** The Big Bang created protons, neutrons, and electrons. Stars assemble these subatomic particles into all the elements of the periodic table.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nucleosynthesis is the process of forming elements. Two students are asked to explain how this process relates to the Big Bang and the life cycle of stars.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which one of these explanations is correct?**

[[☃ radio 1]]


[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: “[Hubble Monitors Supernova In Nearby Galaxy M82](https://images.nasa.gov/details/GSFC_20171208_Archive_e001206)” by NASA Goddard, [NASA Public Domain](https://www.nasa.gov/multimedia/guidelines/index.html)

options.alt: An image of a supernova explosion from another galaxy.
options.caption: *A supernova in another galaxy*

choices.content: **Student A:** Stars are made from hydrogen. The first stars were much more massive than the sun! When they exploded as supernovas, the energy fused the hydrogen atoms into larger elements. This was the Big Bang.

**Student B:** The Big Bang created hydrogen atoms. These hydrogen atoms became the first stars. Stars use nuclear fusion to combine hydrogen atoms into larger elements. Really massive stars create even larger elements when they explode as supernovas.
